input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a 1/10-scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology. Its main hardware components include lower and upper level chassis and autonomy elements. It operates in the automotive domain for Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) systems, characterized by resource-constrained Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that necessitate computation offloading for efficient system performance monitoring and management.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three services: (1) Data Monitoring, which includes real-time visualization of vehicle state data, projected driving paths, and detected object tracks via a user interface, along with data saving and report generation; (2) Computation Offloading, where the ACC system's lead vehicle detection, speed computation based on distance, and actuation control are offloaded from resource-constrained vehicle ECUs to the DT; (3) Remote Management, enabling operators to remotely enable/disable ACC, set desired speeds, and send emergency braking commands, while developers use the system for troubleshooting through data collection and monitoring capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation utilizes Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as the middleware for communication, NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 as the Software Development Kit (SDK) for hardware acceleration, and development tools including Python 3.8, C++, and MATLAB/Simulink for simulation and algorithm development. These technologies collectively support low-latency communication, data collection, and real-time processing required for safety-critical ACC operations.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system requires 100 ms clock synchronization accuracy for safety-critical vehicular applications to enable computation offloading, as mandated by standards and constrained by hardware limitations.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The system employs a hierarchical structure with three distinct entities: Physical Twin Entity (handling data pre-processing, actuation, device communication, and general automotive functionality like Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking), Digital Twin Entity (managing data management and control mechanisms), and User Entity (comprising 3D Visualization and Control Panel components). Each entity is deployed on separate hardware components and implemented as individual ROS2 packages, with each Functional Entity (FE) containing one or more ROS2 nodes. This structure deviates from ISO 23247 RA by redefining the Physical Twin Entity's scope beyond the standard's OME.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin architecture follows ISO 23247 RA, mapping software components to Functional Entities (FEs) implemented as ROS2 packages. Each FE is a ROS2 node or group of nodes, deployed across separate hardware components for Physical Twin, Digital Twin, and User Entities. System-wide coordination is achieved through ROS2 communication, with clock synchronization maintained at 100 ms accuracy for vehicular safety requirements.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models include DBSCAN clustering for raw LiDAR point cloud processing and Kalman filter-based object tracking, with Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) logic for lead vehicle detection and speed computation. Data sources comprise LiDAR point clouds and vehicle state metrics, stored in a SQL database for structured management and real-time analysis.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Vedder Electronic Speed Controller (ESC) converts desired speed into motor voltage commands, enabling precise speed regulation. Emergency braking is implemented via direct control of the vehicle's braking system, with actuation logic designed for collision avoidance scenarios.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Onboard sensors include a Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR for 3D environmental mapping and an Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera for RGB-D imaging, providing synchronized spatial data streams to the Digital Twin for accurate physical representation.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The system maintains real-time, high-fidelity computational reflections with network latency constrained to ≤100 ms. Clock synchronization mechanisms are enforced to ensure data integrity across all components, with validation processes monitoring system performance against fidelity thresholds.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data flows from physical sensors (LiDAR, motor, steering servo, vehicle state) to digital via ROS2 for intra-component communication and Wi-Fi with UDP transport layer for inter-entity communication. Raw point cloud data is processed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking software component, converting it into 2D plane representations. The ISO 23247 Data Translation Functional Element serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for all data streams. Transmitted data types include vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path, displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization interface with preprocessing ensuring data standardization.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The Controlling FE processes specific commands including enabling and disabling ACC, modifying desired speed, and performing emergency braking. These commands are serialized and translated by the Data Translation component, transmitted over ROS2 and Wi-Fi (UDP) networks to the Actuation FE for motor and steering control. Command validation and feedback mechanisms ensure accurate execution and system safety.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication between software components occurs over a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication uses Wi-Fi with UDP transport layer. Network latency is maintained at a maximum of 100 ms as required by system specifications.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin system is applied across the automotive product lifecycle, specifically during the design phase of automotive systems for simulation and validation, during vehicle operation for real-time monitoring and diagnostics, and as a product lifecycle management tool integrating data from development through end-of-life.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support capabilities include Data Monitoring for real-time vehicle state data, Computation Offloading for efficient processing, and Remote Management via a Control Panel. User interaction is facilitated through a user interface (UI), providing insights such as projected driving paths and detected object tracks with confidence measures for recommendations.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliant with ISO 23247:2021 standard, Part 2 (Reference Architecture), defining entity-based structure with Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity. Functional Entities (FEs) may implement interoperability using protocols such as OPC Unified Architecture.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the physical twin as a 1/10-scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with autonomy components. It correctly identifies the hardware layers (lower and upper chassis) and the operational context involving resource-constrained ECUs requiring computation offloading. Quotes: 'physical twin (PT) is a 1/10 [th]-scale autonomous vehicle', 'physical architecture consists of three layers... lower level chassis... upper level chassis... autonomy elements', 'resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient'.

The extraction provides a comprehensive list of the three specific DT services described in the text: Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, and Remote Management. It details the specific capabilities for each, such as real-time visualization, the specific logic offloaded (lead vehicle detection, speed computation), and remote commands. However, it slightly misattributes 'actuation control' to the offloaded components, whereas the text states actuation is carried out on the vehicle after receiving the speed. Quotes: '1) Data Monitoring... display real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and detected object tracks', '2) Computation Offloading... offloading only the ACC system from the vehicle to the DT', '3) Remote Management... enabling/disabling ACC, setting the desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command'.

The description accurately lists the specific software tools and frameworks used (ROS2, NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0, Python 3.8, C++, MATLAB/Simulink) and correctly assigns their roles (middleware, SDK, development tools). It also correctly contextualizes these tools within the requirements of the system, such as low-latency communication and real-time processing for safety-critical operations.

Quotes:
- 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2)... as the middleware to integrate application modules'
- 'We use NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 as the Software Development Kit (SDK) for programming the Jetson Orin Nano'
- 'Application modules are implemented in Python 3.8..., C++..., and MATLAB/Simulink...'

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

We require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.'

Excellent detail describing the three entities (Physical, Digital, User), their specific responsibilities, the implementation hierarchy (ROS2 packages/nodes), and the specific deviation from the ISO 23247 standard regarding the Physical Twin Entity. Supported by quotes: 'User-facing components are organized into the User Entity... SW components that implement data management... are organized into the Digital Twin Entity. Data pre-processing, actuation... are organized into the Physical Twin Entity', 'The existence of this entity marks a departure from the ISO 23247 RA', 'we implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes'.

Comprehensive summary of the architecture, detailing the ISO 23247 mapping, ROS2 implementation, hardware deployment, and system-wide coordination mechanisms including time synchronization. Supported by quotes: 'We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design phase', 'implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package', 'Considering that each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component', 'Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network'.

The source text focuses on the internal architecture and communication protocols (ROS2, Wi-Fi) of the Digital Twin system components. While it mentions dependencies on external resources (cloud compute, external storage), it does not describe specific interoperability standards, integration patterns with enterprise systems, or other Digital Twins as defined in the characteristic description. Thus, stating 'Not in Document' is accurate.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extracted characteristic accurately identifies the computational models used: DBSCAN clustering for raw LiDAR point clouds and Kalman filter-based tracking, as well as ACC logic for lead vehicle detection and speed computation. It also correctly lists the data sources (LiDAR point clouds, vehicle state metrics) and storage method (SQL database). However, it could be expanded with more details on data management, such as the specific roles of 'Data Collection' and 'Data Translation' components. Supported by quotes: 'Clusters raw LiDAR point clouds via DBSCAN, then tracks using a Kalman filter.', 'ACC Determines lead vehicle and computes following speed.', 'Data Storage Gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database.'

The extraction correctly describes the Vedder Electronic Speed Controller (ESC) converting desired speed into motor voltage commands and mentions emergency braking for collision avoidance. However, it omits details about the steering servo actuation and the specific mapping to the 'Actuation FE' in the architecture. Supported by quotes: 'Actuation Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands.', 'perform emergency braking of the ego vehicle.', 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller.'

The extraction accurately lists the Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR for 3D mapping and the Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera for RGB-D imaging, describing their roles in providing spatial data. However, it lacks details on their placement (e.g., 'onboard sensors') and integration with the Physical Twin Entity. Supported by quotes: 'onboard sensors including a Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR, an Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera.', 'Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR, an Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera.'

The extraction correctly states the network latency constraint (≤100 ms) and clock synchronization mechanisms for data integrity. However, it does not mention specific validation processes or fidelity thresholds beyond latency. Supported by quotes: 'The DTS shall maintain network latency of at most 100 ms from the vehicle to the DT Entity.', 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms.', 'Clock synchronization is especially critical in distributed systems for the purposes of event ordering, data integrity, and coordination.'

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. The extraction correctly identifies the specific commands (enabling/disabling ACC, modifying speed, emergency braking) and the involved functional entities (Controlling FE, Actuation FE, Data Translation). Additionally, it asserts the existence of 'Command validation and feedback mechanisms,' which are not detailed in the source documents. While the core control commands and architecture are correct, these unsupported claims regarding validation and specific transport protocol reduce the score.

The extraction correctly summarizes the technical network infrastructure, stating that intra-component communication uses a 'ROS2 network' and inter-entity communication uses a 'Wi-Fi network'. It also correctly identifies the latency requirement of 'at most 100 ms' found in the system requirements. However, like the previous extractions, it specifies 'UDP' transport layer, which is not confirmed in the provided text segment that ends abruptly.

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_5_judge.reasoning: The characteristic accurately reflects the application of Digital Twins across the automotive lifecycle as described in the background section. It correctly identifies the design phase (simulation/validation), vehicle operation (monitoring), and product lifecycle management. Supported by quotes: 'During the design phase of automotive systems, DTs are often used as cyber-physical experimentation facilities', 'During vehicle operation, DTs are used for a variety of optimization and health monitoring purposes', and 'DTs are also used as a product lifecycle management tool... establishing a continuous, end-to-end information flow throughout the lifecycle'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source documents explicitly describe the engineering and development process used for the specific Digital Twin case study. Section IV details the process starting with requirements definition, moving to architectural design, and then implementation. Quotes: 'We now provide a detailed exposition of the case study, starting from the definition of DTS requirements', 'We then elaborate on the architectural design... and the implementation of DTS services'. While it does not cover all aspects like version control, stating it is not in the document is incorrect.

The extraction is highly specific and aligns well with the case study's services. It correctly lists Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, and Remote Management, along with specific UI elements like projected driving paths and object tracks. Supported by quotes: 'Data Monitoring: Monitoring ACC-relevant data... UI allowing a user to view... vehicle state data', 'Computation Offloading: A DT can alleviate the computational burden...', and 'display real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and detected object tracks'. It loses a point for adding 'confidence measures' which are not mentioned in the text.

The extraction accurately identifies the standard ISO 23247:2021, specifically referencing Part 2 (Reference Architecture). It correctly lists the entities defined in the standard (Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity) and mentions interoperability protocols like OPC Unified Architecture. This is highly specific and detailed. Quotes: 'Part 2 of the standard provides a detailed, layered RA', 'The Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)... Data Collection and Device Control Entity... Digital Twin Entity... User Entity', 'one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture (UA)... to facilitate the implementation of the Data Translation FE'.

It discusses data collection and storage (e.g., 'store the ego vehicle state data on a device external to the vehicle') but does not contain any information regarding data ownership policies, privacy protection measures, consent management, or regulatory compliance. While the source text identifies the system as 'safety-critical' and outlines performance requirements relevant to safety such as network latency ('R7.
